coredata.dc:title: White-emitting carbon dots with long alkyl-chain structure: Effective inhibition of aggregation caused quenching effect for label-free imaging of latent fingerprint
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Bang-Ping
Yu, Yun-Xiang
Guo, Xiao-Lu
Ding, Zhao-Yang
Zhou, Bo
Liang, Hong
Shen, Xing-Can
coredata.dc:description: The surface functionalization of carbon dots (CDs) can introduce an additional dimension for the control of their photoluminescence properties and effective promotion of the applications of CDs such as imaging, sensing, etc. In this study, a simple one-step carbonization using Tween 80 as the sole carbon source is described for directly synthesizing white-emitting CDs (WCDs) in high yield. The results revealed the presence of several long alkyl chains on the surface of the as-fabricated WCDs, which effectively suppress the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect and emit bright white luminescence under an UV illumination of 365 nm in the solution and solid states. Besides, together with the surface protection of long alkyl chains, WCDs preferentially interact with lipophilic fatty residues and are beneficial for the label-free imaging of latent fingerprints. This study provides a promising new method to not only effectively inhibit the intrinsic ACQ effect of CDs but also develop the surface engineering of functionalized white-emitting CDs for practical applications via facile synthesis.

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: PL properties of WCDs and their applications for the imaging of latent fingerprints

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Key Laboratory for the Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Key Laboratory of Bioorganic Chemistry and Molecular Engineering
National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the NSFC ( 21671046 , 51562001 , 21364002 and 21502028 ), Guangxi NSFGA (2017GXNSFGA198004, 2013GXNSFGA019001 and 2015GXNSFAA139037 ), Key Laboratory for the Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources ( CMEMR2015-A02, CMEMR2013-A08 , and CMEMR2013-A013 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (a), size distribution (b), XRD pattern (c), and Raman spectrum (d) of the as-prepared WCDs. Inset in a: HR-TEM image of the crystalline lattice of the WCDs. Size histogram (b) of WCDs measured from TEM images for over 100 particles. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
XPS survey (a), C1s (b), O1s (c), and FT-IR (d) spectra of WCDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

1H NMR spectra of WCDs (a) and Tween 80 (b) in DMSO-d 6. Inset: photographs of WCDs in CH2Cl2/H2O system under day light (left) and 365-nm UV light (right). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) PL excitation and emission spectra of WCDs in CH2Cl2. Inset: photographs of WCDs dispersed in CH2Cl2 under day light (left) and 365-nm UV light (right). (b) PL emission spectra of WCDs with different excitation wavelengths from 350 to 510 nm in CH2Cl2. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Solid-state PL spectrum of WCDs, λ
ex = 365 nm. Inset: photographs of solid-power WCDs under dark (left) and 365-nm UV light (right). (b) The chromaticity coordinate of solid-power WCDs on the CIE 1931 chromaticity chart. Images of sebum-rich (c) and sebum-poor (d) fingerprints on microscopic glass stained by WCDs under day light (left) and 365-nm UV light (right) using Biotek Cytation 5. Inset: The second-level details, including bifurcation and crossover. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic illustration of the synthetic route of WCDs.

head.ce:title: White-emitting carbon dots with long alkyl-chain structure: Effective inhibition of aggregation caused quenching effect for label-free imaging of latent fingerprint

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for the Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources, School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Science, Guangxi Normal University, Guilin, 541004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for the Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources
School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The surface functionalization of carbon dots (CDs) can introduce an additional dimension for the control of their photoluminescence properties and effective promotion of the applications of CDs such as imaging, sensing, etc. In this study, a simple one-step carbonization using Tween 80 as the sole carbon source is described for directly synthesizing white-emitting CDs (WCDs) in high yield. The results revealed the presence of several long alkyl chains on the surface of the as-fabricated WCDs, which effectively suppress the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect and emit bright white luminescence under an UV illumination of 365 nm in the solution and solid states. Besides, together with the surface protection of long alkyl chains, WCDs preferentially interact with lipophilic fatty residues and are beneficial for the label-free imaging of latent fingerprints. This study provides a promising new method to not only effectively inhibit the intrinsic ACQ effect of CDs but also develop the surface engineering of functionalized white-emitting CDs for practical applications via facile synthesis.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), representing a fascinating class of recently discovered nanocarbons comprising discrete, quasi-spherical nanoparticles with sizes less than 10 nm, are promising luminescent materials for imaging, sensors, and optoelectronic applications because of their cost-effectiveness, highly stable luminescence, and low toxicity [1–5]. The photoluminescence (PL) mechanism of CDs is explained by two prevalent viewpoints of the surface state in CDs and the quantum size effect [6,7]. Along with the rapid development of CDs-based nanomaterials, CDs with a bare surface only passivated by carboxyl or hydroxyl groups is rather defenseless, and the presence of several factors, such as radical surface interactions and non-radiative states, usually impairs the optoelectronic properties of CDs, severely leading to a low PL efficiency [6,8]. For example, when CDs are dried into films or powders, the undesired occurrence of aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) is observed, which considerably limits their practical applications of their solid-state luminescence [9–12]. Currently, several studies of CDs have been performed to improve their optoelectronic properties [6], especially the inhibition of the ACQ effect [11–14], which is a ubiquitous problem with organic fluorophores [15,16]. By utilizing carboxyl or hydroxyl groups on their surfaces [17,18], polymers or other organic molecules are modified to passivate the CDs surfaces [6,17,19–21], and the PL efficiency of CDs dramatically increases by cutting off other non-emitting routes to amplify the emission intensity produced solely by irradiation [6,22]. For instance, to achieve this purpose, polyethylene glycol [23,24], polyethyleneimine [25–29], and polyhedral oligomeric silsesquioxane [6,30,31] have been used as surface passivation agents. Although such studies offer an exciting opportunity to improve the PL properties of CDs, the methods are often relatively complex and require multiple steps [32], including the synthesis of CDs with carboxyl or hydroxyl groups [17,18] and the grafting of passivation agents [6,17,19–21,30,31,33], etc. Moreover, most of these studies focus on the effect of surface passivation on solution-state PL behavior, but solid-state PL behavior is rarely involved [12–14,34,35]. Solid-state PL is crucial for practical applications, such as sensors and optoelectronic devices, and so forth [36,37]. Hence, it is still important to develop a facile one-step synthetic method for CDs with surface protection and further investigate their solid-state PL behavior.

In addition, traditional CDs only capped by carboxyl or hydroxyl groups cannot satisfy for some specific applications [1–5,38]. Thus, it is an urgent need to introduce new features and properties for CDs via the introduction of different surface functional groups [6,38]. For example, because of the hydrophilic nature of the considerable amount of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface, the majority of the previously reported CDs cannot serve as lipophilic luminescent probes, definitely limiting their applications in hydrophobic media and surfaces [34,38–42]. Lipophilic luminescent probes exert considerable importance for several applications, such as labeling hydrophobic microdomains, tracing live cells, etc [40,43]. The unique luminescent property and excellent biocompatibility of CDs have prompted researchers to develop lipophilic CDs [39–43]. Compared to the synthetic methods for hydrophilic CDs, there are not only seldom reports on the synthesis of lipophilic CDs, but also these methods suffer from limitations like rare carbon sources, complicated preparation processes and harsh preparation conditions [41,43]. Therefore, the development of facile, green methods for synthesizing lipophilic CDs has been attracting immense interest.

Fingerprints have been utilized as one of the typical techniques for personal identification for forensic examination, law enforcement, access control, and medical diagnostics [44–47]. Fingerprints are typically not visible to the naked eye; thus, one or several appropriate physical or chemical techniques are required for detection and visualization [44,48], including powder dusting, fluorescent dye staining, cyanoacrylate/iodine fuming, and vacuum metal deposition, etc [49]. Visualization is possible because fingerprints contain a variable mixture of water, fatty acids, salts, and free amino acids [50–53]. The methods via the interaction with these components, especially the selective attack of the amino acids or fatty residues [50], provide some contrast between the surface and fingerprint ridges, leading to the enhancement of latent fingerprints [50–53]. Among various detection techniques, fluorescence staining is a general method for the detection of latent fingerprints with high sensitivity [49,54]. CDs, as one type of cost-effective, highly luminescent-stable, low-toxicity nanomaterials, are expected to be applied for the visualization of latent fingerprints [48,55–58]. In fact, several obstacles still hinder the application of CDs-based nanomaterials for the visualization of latent fingerprints [48]. For example, the PL of CDs is always quenched by their solid-state aggregation because of the ACQ effect, which is extremely weak to identify the latent fingerprints by naked eyes [48,55]. Moreover, typical CDs lack surface groups that can interact with fingerprint residues, leading to the absence of stable adherence of the CDs on the latent fingerprint surface [48,59].

In this study, we report a facile one-step route to prepare white-emitting CDs (WCDs) with a large number of long alkyl chains as the protecting groups on the surface via a high-yield one-step carbonization of polyoxyethylene sorbitan monooleate (Tween 80) (Scheme 1 ), which are applied for the label-free imaging of latent fingerprints. In our systems, one-step carbonization permits the facile preparation of lipophilic CDs. More importantly, the long alkyl-chain characteristic of WCDs renders the label-free visualization of latent fingerprints via preferential hydrophobic interactions with fatty residues of fingerprints; meanwhile, as protecting groups, the long alkyl chains avoid the PL quenching of WCDs in the solid state. Besides, white-emitting CD-based nanomaterials demonstrate immense potential for various lighting applications, especially for white-light-emitting diodes (W-LEDs) [60–62]. As a single-component entity and heavy-metal ion-free white-light-emitting nanomaterials, the obtained WCDs may be not only expected to prevent the drawbacks of combined emitters, thereby producing high-quality white light, but also thought to be an environment-friendly alternative for conventional phosphors.

As depicted in Scheme 1, WCDs were prepared by the one-step carbonization of Tween 80 using concentrated sulfuric acid and phosphoric acid as the oxidizing agent at 90 °C for 3 h, affording a yellow powder in 18%; this value is greater than the typical yields observed for previously reported WCDs [1–5]. Typically, CDs are obtained in low yield because of the complex reaction, aggregation during carbonization, difficult separation and purification, etc [63]. The low yield is still one of the major drawbacks that have severely limited future biological applications of CDs. In particular, for previously reported WCDs, their yields are even lower than those of commonly reported CDs [62]. The as-prepared WCDs can effectively dissolve in various organic solvents, including chloroform, dichloromethane (CH2Cl2), acetone, etc, and exhibit a long-term homogeneous phase without any clear sediment at room temperature for several months, facilitating their application and storage.

After the synthesis, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurement was carried out to primarily characterize the morphology and size distribution of WCDs. As-prepared WCDs are spherical (Fig. 1 a), indicating that WCDs comprise nanodots and exhibit a uniform dispersion without apparent aggregation, as well as a relatively narrow size distribution between 3.5 and 5.3 nm with the maximum population centered at ∼4 nm (Fig. 1b). Meanwhile, gel permeation chromatography experiments of WCDs revealed molecular weights ranging from 0.5 to 2 kDa (Fig. S1). The ratio of the weight-average and number-average molecular weights of the as-prepared WCDs is ∼1.3, indicating that WCDs exhibit a narrow molecular distribution. This assumes that nanoparticles serve as a single entity. High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) measurement (Fig. 1a, Inset) revealed that the WCDs exhibit a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the [100] facet of graphitic carbon and not the typical interlayer distance of graphite ([002], 0.34 nm) [11]. The [002] interlayer distance of the graphite structure was supported by X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis, where a broad peak centered at 2θ = 23° (0.34 nm) is observed (Fig. 1c). In addition, Raman spectroscopy measurement (Fig. 1d) was carried out to confirm the presence of the graphite-like structure in the WCDs. Two major bands at 1356 cm−1 (D-band) and 1592 cm−1 (G-band) are observed, corresponding to the vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds of the disordered sp3 carbon structure and sp2-bonded carbon atoms in a 2D hexagonal lattice of a graphite cluster, respectively. The D and G band intensity ratio is an important index of the defect density in the CD structure [11]. The intensity ratio of the D and G bands is 0.85, suggesting that sp2 carbons are mainly present, with some sp3 hybrid carbons in WCDs.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to examine the surface functional groups and element states of WCDs. As can be observed from the XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 2 a), two clear peaks are centered at 285.3 and 533.3 eV, corresponding to C1s and O1s, respectively. The C1s spectrum of WCDs is fitted into four components, corresponding to CC at 284.2 eV, CC at 284.8 eV, CO at 286.1 eV, and CO at 289.3 eV (Fig. 2b). In the O1s spectrum (Fig. 2c), the signals at 532.2, 533.3, and 534.1 eV confirm the presence of the CO, CO, and OH functional groups. The presence of such characteristic functional groups on the WCD surface was confirmed by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy. WCDs exhibit characteristic absorption peaks at 966 and 1297 cm−1 (Fig. 2d), corresponding to the symmetric stretching vibrations of CO group, and at 1709 cm−1, corresponding to the CO bending vibration. In addition, peaks are observed at 2922 and 2853 cm−1, corresponding to the CH stretching, and at 3436 cm−1, corresponding to the OH stretching vibration. These observations of functional groups on the WCD surface are in good agreement with those of CDs reported previously [1–5].

To gain further insights into the chemical structure of WCDs, nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra were recorded. In the 1H NMR spectrum (Fig. 3 a), the presence of the CC group in the WCDs is confirmed by the peak at 5.4 ppm. Besides, Fig. 3a also reveals a large number of alkyl chains on the WCD surface, evidenced by the main peak at 1.3 ppm and a peak at 0.8 ppm, corresponding to the protons of CH2 and CH3 groups, respectively. Compared to that of WCDs, not only signals of hydrogen atoms on the CC group and alkyl chain, but also those corresponding to the characteristic proton signal of the OCH2CH2O− group around 3.5 ppm are observed in the 1H NMR spectrum of Tween 80 (Fig. 3b). These results revealed that (i) the carbonization of Tween 80 leads to the transformation of the OCH2CH2O− group into the CD core, and (ii) the long alkyl chains of Tween 80 are retained on the surface of the as-prepared WCDs after carbonization. Moreover, the 13C NMR spectra also confirm the presence of the CC groups and alkyl chains (Fig. S2). Fig. 3 (Inset) shows the photographs of WCDs in the CH2Cl2/H2O system. Under day light, only the CH2Cl2 layer exhibits a pale brown color, and upon exposure to 365-nm UV light, a bright white color is observed for the CH2Cl2 layer and not the aqueous layer. This result indicates that the surface groups of long-alkyl chains make WCDs lipophilic and not hydrophilic, which adds new characteristics and properties for CDs to applications in hydrophobic media and surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL properties of WCDs and their applications for the imaging of latent fingerprints
ce:section.ce:para: The unique optical performance is one of the most fascinating properties of CDs, offering immense potential for future applications in imaging, optoelectronic devices, etc. The UV–Vis spectrum of WCDs in CH2Cl2 was recorded. An obvious absorption peak around 298 nm is observed, with a tail extending into the visible range, corresponding to the n−π* transition of nanocarbon (Fig. S3) [60]. This phenomenon has been observed for a wide range of luminescent CDs. Next, the PL characteristics of WCDs were evaluated. With excitation at the maximum excitation of 363 nm, a strong emission peak at 435 nm is observed, with a shoulder peak around 510 nm (Fig. 4 a), corresponding to white luminescent emission. The quantum yield in solution (QYsolution) is determined to be 2.1% by an absolute method; meanwhile, a relative method is also used to measure the QYsolution of WCDs (QYsolution = 4.2%) (Table S1). The QY values obtained by absolute and relative methods are close to each other. Notably, the QY of the WCDs is relatively low, but it is comparable to those reported previously [62,65]. The low QY of our WCDs is possibly related to the synthetic method and intrinsic characteristics of WCDs [11,64]. The remarkable white luminescence under 365-nm UV irradiation further demonstrates the white-emitting feature of WCDs (Fig. 4a, inset). Moreover, excitation-dependent PL behavior is also observed. With the increase in the excitation wavelength from 350 to 510 nm, the emission peak red-shifts from 440 to 550 nm (Fig. 4b), contributing to the surface state that affects the WCD band gap. Typically, in CD materials, where the surface groups, such as CO, CO, and OCOH, can introduce trapping states with different energy levels, thereby varying the light emission of CDs with excitation energy [1–5,38,66,67]. Fluorescence lifetime measurements were also carried out to assess their PL properties. Multiple lifetimes are observed (Fig. S4), which indicates that several energy levels are present in WCDs, leading to the aforementioned tunable PL behavior.

White-emitting feature, together with long alkyl-chain structure, provides diverse possibilities for WCDs to be further used in various applications. For instance, the long alkyl chains on the WCD surface may effectively inhibit their ACQ effect and maintain excellent luminescent emission, which is vital for aggregation-state or solid-state device molecules to satisfy the requirements for practical applications, including fingerprint detection, light phosphors, etc. Fig. 5 a shows the solid-state PL spectrum of WCDs. Upon excitation at 365 nm, an optimal emission band is observed at 455 nm, which is also broad and spans the entire width of the visible spectrum. Under 365-nm UV excitation, solid WCD emits white fluorescence (Fig. 5a, Inset), with a CIE 1931 chromaticity coordinate of (0.27, 0.31) (Fig. 5b), which is similar to that of pure white light (0.33, 0.33). The measured solid-state QY (QYsolid) of the WCDs is 2.0%; this value is almost identical to that of solution-state WCDs (QYsolution = 2.1%). These results indicated that WCDs can emit white light in both solution and solid states, and the long alkyl-chain structure of WCDs effectively prevents the undesired ACQ effect, thereby retaining the excellent PL activity of WCDs in the solid state. To gain insights into the relationship between composition, structure, and properties, tween reagents with different alkyl-chain lengths (polyethylene glycol sorbitan monolaurate, Tween 20; polyoxyethylene sorbitan monopalmitate, Tween 40) were selected to prepare another two kinds of CDs with different alkyl-chain lengths. The results revealed that CDs prepared by Tween 20 and Tween 40 exhibit white PL emission (Fig. S5−S7), which is similar to the emission characteristics of WCDs prepared by Tween 80, also denoted as WCDs. This result further confirmed that the WCD core is converted from the OCH2CH2O− group of tween reagents. The OCH2CH2O− groups of Tween 20, Tween 40, and Tween 80 are identical, thereby leading to similar PL emission. Moreover, the QYsolution values of these WCDs are almost identical to each other (Table S1), whereas the QYsolid showed a little difference. QYsolid values are 1.7%, 1.9%, and 2.0% for WCDs prepared by Tween 20, Tween 40, and Tween 80 using the absolute method (Table S1). These observations revealed that the alkyl-chain length of WCDs does not affect the QYsolution, but slightly affects their QYsolid. In the solid state, WCDs were aggregated. The longer alkyl chains on the WCD surface possibly makes the as-prepared WCDs suppress the ACQ effect more effectively, thereby increasing the QYsolid as the alkyl-chain length on the WCD surface raises. It should be pointed out that the QYsolid in our system just slightly increases as the alkyl-chain length on the surface of WCDs raises. This was probably because even the shortest alkyl chain produced from Tween 20 can also effectively inhibit the ACQ effect of WCDs, making that the difference of the inhibition effect resulted from different alkyl chain lengths was not obvious.

Because of the excellent solid-state PL emission and lipophilic features of WCDs, WCDs are used to enhance the visualization of latent fingerprints by their solid-state PL. Fig. 5c shows the PL images of sebaceous fingerprints (sebum-rich) developed by WCDs. Sebaceous fingerprints were obtained by volunteers gently rubbing their fingertips over the forehead or nose, followed by stamping them on the microscopic glass with minimal pressure. The glass was immersed in the ethanol/water solution of the WCDs under stirring for 15 min, followed by washing with water and drying under air. Photographs were taken under day light or 365-nm UV light using a multi-mode microplate reader (Biotek Cytation 5). Apparently, the fingerprint patterns are clearly resolved, in which the papillary ridges are lighted up under UV illumination and designated by white color. For example, the second level of information, including bifurcation and crossover, is distinguished in the PL images of fingerprints, which are used to identify the individuals (Fig. 5c). As a control experiment, the images of sebum-poor fingerprints are recorded. Compared to the optical and PL images of WCD-adhered sebaceous fingerprints (Fig. 5c), those of sebum-poor ones are not clear to enable the identification of the individuals via the ridge pattern of the fingerprint (Fig. 5d). In addition, when photographs are recorded with a common digital camera, a lower background interference is observed for the sebaceous ones, where the luminescence of fingerprints is sufficiently strong such that it can even overcome the reflection light from the blue background luminescence from glass (Fig. S8). These details form the basis of fingerprint identification in principle, which are critical in practical applications [48]. Such results confirm our hypothesis that the long alkyl-chain structure on the WCD surface can promote the hydrophobic interaction between WCDs and fatty residues of latent fingerprints, enhancing the visualization of latent fingerprints. Besides, the benign white-emitting characteristic of WCDs endows immense potential applications for white-light-emitting diodes (W-LEDs). A WCD-based emitting device is fabricated, where WCD/silicone mixtures coated on a 365-nm UV chip serve as a white-light converter (Fig. S9, Inset). The as-fabricated device produces bright-white light with a color rendering index of 76.2 at 350 mA, which is comparable to that of commercial W-LEDs [65]. The color character of the luminescence from this device is realized at (0.31, 0.30) by the analysis of the CIE 1931 chromaticity coordinate (Fig. S9), and a CCT of 7034 K is observed, indicative of the cool white-light characteristics of the as-fabricated device. Our WCDs, as a single-component entity and heavy-metal-ion-free white-light-emitting nanomaterials, may be expected to not only avoid the drawbacks of combined emitters, thereby leading to high-quality white light, but also serve as an environment-friendly alternative for conventional phosphors.

In conclusion, we present a simple one-step carbonization process using Tween 80 as the sole carbon source to directly synthesize white-emitting CDs, with a high product yield of 18%. The characterization results demonstrate that there are many long alkyl chains on the surface of the as-fabricated WCDs. Benefitting from such long alkyl-chain structure, (i) they can effectively suppress the ACQ effect, and emit bright white luminescence under 365-nm UV illumination in both solution and solid state, (ii) they can preferentially interact with lipophilic substance, such as fatty residues. Based on these characteristics, they are applied for the label-free luminescent imaging of latent fingerprints, where the compelling results are observed. The present research may not only provide a promising new method to obtain high yield of white-emitting CDs, but also opens the door to develop CDs with surface groups protection for diverse practical applications.

Concentrated phosphoric acid and sulfuric acid were obtained from Sinopharm chemical reagent co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Tween 20, Tween 40, and Tween 80 were purchased from acros organics (Shanghai, China). All reagents were used without further purification.

10 mL Tween 80 was mixed with concentrated phosphoric acid (30 mL) and concentrated sulfuric acid (10 mL) in a round-bottom flask (100 mL). The mixture was stirred at 90 °C for 3 h. after cooling to room temperature, a large number of black solid was precipitated. The resultant solution was filtered to remove unreacted Tween 80 and a majority of acids. Subsequently, the precipitate was rinsed with amount of water to further remove residual acids and other water-soluble substances. Finally, after dried in a vacuum, a pale yellow power was obtained in 18% yield.
Under the same condition, WCDs with different alkyl-chain lengths were also prepared from Tween 20 and Tween 40, respectively.

UV–Vis spectrum was recorded on an agilent technologies cary 100 UV–Vis spectrometer. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was taken on a Rigaku-D/max 2500v/pc diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan) equipped with graphite monochromatized Cu Kα (40 kV, 150 mA) radiation at a scanning speed of 4° min−1 in the range from 4 to 90°. Raman spectroscopy was investigated by a Renishaw invia Raman microscope at 514 nm exciting line, which were taken with 10 s of exposure times and 30% of power at 3 times of accumulation. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was collected using a Bruker D&Advance X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectra were collected on a Bruker AVANCE AV500 spectrometer using DMSO-d 6 as solvent. Additionally, the Fourier transformed infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was obtained on a Perkin Klmer PE spectrum one FT-IR spectrometer. Fluorescence spectra were measured on a Shimadzu RF-5301PC luminescence spectrometer. The morphology of the WCDs was characterized by TEM, which was performed on a Hitachi H-600 field-emission transmission electron microscope with accelerating voltage of 200 kV. the fluorescence lifetime measurements were recorded on a Horiba Jobin Jvon FL3-P-TCSPS luminescence spectrometer.

The GPC measurement was performed on a HP1100 gel permeation chromatographer. 5 mg mL−1 THF solution of WCDs was obtained by taking a certain amount of WCDs in THF, and filtered with ultrafiltration membrane. The resultant solution (50 μL) was injected into chromatographic column, and washed with THF.

The absolute QY was measured, referring to the procedure in the literature [16]. It was recorded on an Edinburgh Analytical Instruments FLS920 with an inner diameter of 150 mm integrating sphere coated inside with BaSO4 and calculated by using following equation:
Φ=
f α
=
∫
L
emission
∫
E
slovent
-
∫
E
sample
where f is the photons emitted by the sample; α is the photons absorbed by the sample; QY is the absolute quantum yield; L
emission is the luminescence emission spectrum of the sample, collected using the sphere; E
sample is the spectrum of the light used to excite the sample, collected using the sphere; and E
solvent is the spectrum of the light used for excitation with only the solvent in the sphere, collected using the sphere. Sample solution of WCDs was used to measure QY in CH2Cl2 at 25 °C, λex = 363 nm.

The relative QY was measured, referring to the procedure in the literature [68]. Quinine sulfate (QY = 54% in 0.1 M H2SO4) was selected as the reference. The QY of a sample was then calculated according to the following equation:
ϕ =
ϕ ′
×
A ′
I ′
×
I A
×
n 2
n
′ 2
where ϕ is the QY of the testing sample, I is the testing sample's integrated emission intensity, n is the refractive index (1.33 for water and 1.42 for CH2Cl2), and A is the optical density. The superscript “´” refers to quinine sulfate. To obtain more reliable results, a series of solutions of WCDs and quinine sulfate were prepared with concentrations adjusted such that the optical absorbance values were between 0 and 0.1 at 365 nm (or other desired). The PL spectra were measured and the PL intensity was integrated. QY was determined by comparison of the integrated PL intensity vs absorbance curves (refractive index, n, had also to be considered).

The visualization of latent fingerprints was measured, as follows: for sebaceous fingerprints (sebum-rich): before fingerprints were collected, volunteers should be cleaned up their fingers with soap. Subsequently, they gently rubbed their fingertips over the forehead or nose, and stamped them on microscopic glass with a minimal pressure to leave fingermarks that contain sebaceous materials. The substrate was swayed for an appropriate period of time (5 min) in the ethanol–water (4:1) mixtures of WCDs, then gently rinsed with water twice or thrice. The images of the latent fingerprints arose under daylight or UV light illumination at 365 nm, and photographs were recorded with a common digital camera.

For sebum-poor fingerprints: Before fingerprints were collected, volunteers should be cleaned up their fingers with soap. Subsequently, they kept their fingertips not touching any object for 10 min, and then stamped them on microscopic glass with a minimal pressure to leave fingermarks that contain sebum-poor materials. The substrate was swayed for an appropriate period of time (5 min) in the ethanol–water (4:1) mixtures of WCDs, then gently rinsed with water twice or thrice. The images of the latent fingerprints arose under daylight or UV light illumination at 365 nm, and photographs were recorded with multi-mode microplate reader (Biotek Cytation 5) or common digital camera.

The white-emitting device was prepared, referring to the procedure in the literature [65]. For the fabrication of white-emitting device, a UV-LED chip with the peak wavelength centered at ∼365 nm was attached on the bottom of the LED base. The two leads on device were prepared to connect to the power supply. Afterward, the thermo curable resin (silicone, Dow corning co.) was mixed with WCDs phosphor (WCDs/silicone = 1/10 wt/wt) and put in a vacuum chamber to remove the bubbles. The WCD/silicone mixtures were dispensed on the LED chip and thermally cured at 150 °C for 1 h. all the optical performances were measured using a SPEC300 instrument with integral sphere.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the NSFC (21671046, 51562001, 21364002 and 21502028), Guangxi NSFGA (2017GXNSFGA198004, 2013GXNSFGA019001 and 2015GXNSFAA139037), Key Laboratory for the Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources (CMEMR2015-A02, CMEMR2013-A08, and CMEMR2013-A013).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
GPC, 13C NMR, UV–Vis spectra and time-resolved fluorescence decay curve of WCDs, as well as the chromaticity coordinate of the WCDs-based white-emitting device on the CIE 1931 chromaticity chart.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2017.11.070.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.
